qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Unauthenticated internal control-plane RPCs allow remote shard takeover and deletion
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory targets `common/security/tls.go` `trustedCertPool()` mishandling of multi-certificate PEM CA bundles during mTLS validation, while this finding is a separate missing-authentication issue on privileged internal/admin gRPC listeners.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Predictable public session identifiers allow unauthorized session closure and session-bound write hijack
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory is about TLS CA-bundle parsing and certificate-chain validation in `trustedCertPool()`, while this finding is a public session-ownership failure that trusts attacker-supplied session identifiers.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described root cause in `trustedCertPool()` where multi-certificate PEM bundles were mishandled during CA loading for mTLS. Both qualifying findings are distinct authorization defects on RPC/session surfaces.
evidence: The advisory summary and details tie the vulnerable code to `common/security/tls.go` `trustedCertPool()` only decoding the first PEM block from a CA bundle, breaking certificate-chain validation for mTLS.

`session_id_hijack` is grounded in public session metadata exposure and caller-controlled `session_id` authorization across `client.proto`, `public_rpc_server.go`, and `session_manager.go`, not in TLS or certificate parsing.

`internal_control_plane_no_auth` is grounded in unauthenticated privileged gRPC listeners and missing RPC authorization on internal/admin control-plane methods, not in CA-bundle handling or mTLS chain validation.
The session checklist pruned the RPC-auth/TLS family as having no independent issuer/audience or certificate-validation bypass beyond the separate accepted access-control findings.
